senses.definition: a genus of small annelid worms that live on the surfaces of crayfish and other crustaceans.
examples: Branchiobdella species attach to crayfish gills and exoskeletons.
Researchers studied the relationship between branchiobdella and their crayfish hosts.

senses.definition: relating to the branchial (gill) arches and the heart, often used in anatomical contexts.
examples: Branchiocardiac development is studied in embryology.
The branchiocardiac region shows distinct neural innervation.

senses.definition: originating from or produced by the branchial arches or gill-related structures.
examples: Branchiogenous tissues contribute to the development of the pharyngeal region.
Researchers described branchiogenous derivatives in shark embryos.

senses.definition: one of the segmental blocks (metameres) of the branchial region in the embryos of air-breathing vertebrates.
examples: Branchiomeres contribute to the formation of the gill arches.
Developmental scientists map branchiomere positions during early embryogenesis.

senses.definition: relating to the branchial (gill) arches and the nerves that arise from them.
examples: Branchiomeric nerves include some cranial nerves important for facial movement.
The study described branchiomeric muscle development in embryos.

senses.definition: the arrangement or pattern of branchial arches or nerves during development.
examples: Branchiomerism patterns help classify vertebrate embryos.
Differences in branchiomerism explain variations in cranial nerve layouts.

senses.definition: relating to the branchial (gill) chamber and pallial (mantle or surrounding) region, used in some crustacean anatomy terms.
examples: Branchiopallial features are studied in crustacean respiration.
The term appears in specialized anatomical descriptions.

senses.definition: relating to branchial (gill) respiration or respiration through gills in certain organisms.
examples: Branchiopneustic species rely on gill-based respiration.
Branchiopneustic mechanisms are discussed in comparative physiology.

senses.definition: a group of crustaceans that have a branchial filter and many legs; typically used for small, aquatic crustaceans such as water fleas and their relatives.
examples: Branchiopods include fairy shrimps and water fleas.
Researchers collected samples of branchiopods from temporary ponds.

senses.definition: a class of crustaceans that includes brine shrimp, water fleas, and their relatives.
examples: Branchiopoda is a diverse group of small aquatic crustaceans.
The fossil record shows ancient Branchiopoda.

senses.definition: pertaining to Branchiopoda or its members.
examples: Branchiopodan anatomy can be studied with microscopy.
Branchiopodan fossils provide insight into crustacean evolution.

senses.definition: having features of branchiopods or resembling branchiopods.
examples: Branchiopodous gills were observed in the specimen.
The branchiopodous morphology suggested aquatic lifestyle.

senses.definition: plural of branchiopod; small aquatic crustaceans including Daphnia and Artemia.
examples: Branchiopods are common in temporary ponds.
Researchers used branchiopods as model organisms in ecology.

senses.definition: informal or jocular term, not widely recognized in formal dictionaries; may denote a fictional or playful creature related to branchiopods.
examples: In the comic, the branchiopoo is a tiny aquatic creature.

senses.definition: a proposed grouping of branchiopod crustaceans that are linked to lung-bearing (pulmonate) features in some classifications.
examples: Branchiopulmonata is used in some evolutionary schemes for crustaceans.

senses.definition: having branchiopod characteristics and pulmonate (lung-related) features.
examples: Branchiopulmonate organisms show both gill-like and lung-like structures.

senses.definition: an extinct reptile from the Paleozoic era with branchial (gill-like) features in some depictions or reconstructions; historically used in discussions of early amphibian-like reptiles.
examples: Branchiosaur fossils shed light on the transition from aquatic to terrestrial life.

senses.definition: an extinct order or grouping of small Paleozoic organisms related to branchiosaurs.
examples: Branchiosauria is discussed in early amphibian evolution literature.

senses.definition: relating to branchiosaurs or branchiosauria.
examples: Branchiosaurian features are debated among paleontologists.

senses.definition: a genus name historically used for small, branchiostegally structured Paleozoic creatures; now often considered a taxonomic synonym or reclassified.
examples: Branchiosaurus has appeared in early illustrations of amphibian evolution.

senses.definition: relating to branchial gill arches and the delicate bones or membranes (stegites) near the throat area in some fishes and other aquatic vertebrates.
examples: Branchiostegal rays support the gill membranes.
Anatomy texts describe branchiostegal bones in bony fishes.

senses.definition: relating to branchiosteges or branchial gill structures; used in anatomy.
examples: Branchiostegan features were noted in the fossil specimen.

senses.definition: a branchial or gill-related plate or element in certain anatomical contexts.
examples: The branchiostege forms part of the gill cover.

senses.definition: a family name in crustacean or anatomical nomenclature referring to branchiostegal structures.
examples: Branchiostegidae is cited in taxonomic classifications.

senses.definition: a plate or shield near the gill region in crustaceans; part of the exoskeleton covering branchial structures.
examples: Branchiostegite plates protect delicate gill filaments.
